item.title: Prophetic?
item.content:encoded:
Before reading the actual plaque I would've guessed it was the famous (supposedly) Einstein's quote about nuclear weapons: "I do not know with what weapons World War III will be fought, but World War IV will be fought with sticks and stones."
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Further than that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So… those who see the whole thing directly are bombed back to the Stone Age?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's really not an incredibly important part of the SCP, and it's not even a deciding factor in my vote, but it's not about blast direction or blast shadow. During the detonations during the Manhattan project, the ambient pressure changed so much that even outside of the actual blast zone, the internal pressure of closed environments versus the external pressure changed so drastically that brittle substances, especially flat panes of glass, burst into ball bearing sized projectiles before the shockwave hit. That's where they projected the highest amount of casualties to be created, from wood, glass, and loose objects being super-accelerated into lethal projectiles. Granted, they would have died from the radiation anyway, but when ambient pressure is changed so drastically, it doesn't matter which direction it's coming from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not so fast- if you're looking toward the blast in a modern major city, 1/4 of the glass would be in the blast shadow of reinforced concrete buildings like skyscrapers or large blocks of structures. So if you're far enough away that people are being burned rather than turned into paste, you're going to see glass getting broken rather than vaporized in at least some of the windows facing toward you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate to be this guy, but I have a small gripe.
The author states that the painting is incredibly accurate to the point of reflections in the glass shards.

Having done some research for post education based on the effects of nuclear testing and Hiroshima and Nagasaki, there can be no such thing. If the people being portrayed are being engulfed in the firestorm, the glass visible from this area would be introduced to levels of pressure that wouldn't produce shards. The glass would be shattered into high velocity pebbles, very sharp projectiles, but nothing large enough to produce a reflection. It's not really important, I know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seems like it plays the beats of SCP-804, only in more metaphoric (and to a degree, quite heavy-handed) and ultimately less artistic way.

This is one of the cases where my downvote is ultimately cumulative: I can see remaining neutral if the note wasn't there or if the note was there but the rest of the skip was more elegant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tend to like AWCY? things, and this was no exception. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest i up-voted mainly because of the way the actual painting was described… so basically one sentence sold it for me.

Extremely detailed oil paintnig of a nuke going off in a dense city, glass windows being exploded outwards, the shards showing the reflections of the people in the street as the atomic firestorm rushes forward to devour them.

Sorry just wanted to try.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one's a little tough for me. On the one hand, I like AWCY? and this really works for them. On the other, the skip as a whole isn't super interesting. I think the ending plaque is enough for an upvote, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You could say this painting makes the crowd truly go wild.

…Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
there's no possible way I can buy into total body reconstruction upon seeing a painting

This.

Are We Cavemen Yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah. I was already kinda unimpressed. Pretty soon I was pre-cringeing, waiting for the AWCY shoe to drop, and lo, it dropped. Seemed quite forced, and out of character for them as you say.

Hell, I like AWCY, at least seemingly more than many people. But this didn't do it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that the "all anomalous art = AWCY?" thing has kind of subsided, it seems odd to have this be an AWCY? article. It's not particularly guerrilla art-ish, which is, at least in part, AWCY?'s schtick. In addition, viewing it doesn't change your perceptions. Well, it does insofar as it turns you into a caveman, but c'mon…

Still my least favorite AWCY? article. I do like the idea of Research Assistant Dustin Hoffman counting every single glass shard, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, thank you that clears things up a bit :D
must admit I didn't read carefully enough…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Detailed descriptions of the item were obtained by covering up a small portion of it, as specified in the containment procedures. Furthermore, MTF Upsilon-23 ("Art Critics") are specially designated to deal with AWCY? items, and are well-equipped and trained to handle visual hazards. They're experts! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad at all, though it makes me wonder how they ever described the paintings without looking at it to avoid its effect (well, previous discussion attributed that to mr. autistic researcher…), or how they ever confiscated it in first place. Putting a delay on the effect would have made things smoother I guess…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…The people killed by the exposed patrons were killed via bludgeoning, cannibalism and force copulation? That's pretty horrifying right there.

That being said, the rest of it, while detailed, isn't very interesting, and (while this is probably your intention) the "artist's" motive was very ham-handed.

Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The majority of humankind is no better than our ancestral species. But that's okay, we have sticks that launch rocks and fire, which gives us the illusion that we're somehow superior and advanced.

+1 for Painting What Makes True Colors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's exactly what I was hoping to accomplish, thank you very much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that this is the first time an AWCY reference actually bumped it *into* an upvote for me, I'm sort of neutral on the object/effect itself (though I applaud the level of detail used), it's the fact that this is the first AWCY I've seen done right since the glut of "random thing that does something ironic or outright unrelated, on and Are We Cool Yet?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure, but I think you've written an AWCY? item that reflects a realistic artistic intent/philosophy.
Isn't there some sort of rule against that?

Bonus points for the plague language that completly fits artist-ese while making the catch phrase sound organic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool story, but there's no possible way I can buy into total body reconstruction upon seeing a painting :( the writing and overall message of the story is great though, so a no-vote from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, but in painting what Australopithecizes you, with a dash of AWCY? thrown in for good measure? No, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahh ok it makes sense, thanks for the clarification. Seems a bit rash for me to point those out. For the subtlety made by 1) I would attribute it to good writing ability (I'm not good at reading between the lines >.<)

For everything you have done, I'll give it a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Assistant Researcher Autism Man has been awarded a Foundation Medal for his tireless work spent counting the gears in SCP-914."

"Addendum: Assistant Researcher Autism Man has informed that his medal has 358 bumps around its edge, and as he prefers odd numbers and the color blue, he has returned it."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Must've skimmed over that part. I was thinking perhaps it had to do with the line stating one has to view the entire object to be affected by it, and so if someone was up close, counting bits, it wouldn't work. But that seems dangerous, so I wanted clarification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) They don't know who painted it. The artist it was attributed to didn't do it, that was a ruse to get people to view it. (Read the footnotes.)

2) I could add more details on security, I suppose, but it's pretty easy to just shoot the caveman.

3) It's clearly stated that exposed subjects are to be terminated after testing is concluded. That's because, as stated, efforts at re-educating and rehabilitating victims have failed. The Foundation tried their best to help 'fix' victims of the painting, but met with no success… So now they just shoot them, put them out of their misery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this, upvoting. That being said, I think the details of the painting would read better as a bulleted or numbered list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Execution is alright, concept is, I have to admit, having a hint of freshness in it, but there are somethings that might bug me or help improve the SCP

1) What happens to the man who painted the thing, if this was being showcased, it means the artist should be known with the administration required for a showcasing and the foundation is not going to let such a person just run around potentially creating more SCP's, not even amnesiac would stop a person from re-doing what he did.

2) Details on what happens during exposure like how many guards and so on would help researchers know what to do if they test while, as a article, show just how bad this thing can be. As of right now, I see "Angrier and stronger" but by how much? I know a caveman is stronger, but one's definition and another is different. Remember, your mileage may vary and the foundation would do all it can to reduce subjectivity and put strict numbers to ensure nothing goes wrong.

3) What happens to those expose? Keeping them for further research or terminating them? If you ask me, further research seems to be the better route, at least until they prove that they are just regular caveman with nothing else of interest, than termination would seem viable

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't it clearly state you can safely view the subect remotely, so Assitant Researcher Autism Man is completely uneccecary?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm fine with taking this to PM, and I see that I have one. Please see my PM, and I will discontinue this discussion here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
who is the Assistant Researcher Autism Man who counted all the fucking windows and people

I laughed out loud at this.

I want to write a tale about this now. Research Assistant that that writes normal reports, except with specific attention to incredibly small details for seemingly no reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, that must have been a shit job! As long as he didn't see the whole painting at any one time, he'd have been fine, though.

The Foundation would have counted the number of people for the same reason they compared the skyline to existing cities: So they'd be able to tell if this event was predicted, should it occur in the future. You're right that there's no reason for a precise count of broken glass though, I'll remove that number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay after looking around I'm going to go ahead and say this advice is bad and probably untrue, given that semicolons happen regularly in books, magazines, newspapers, etc. I assume SOME/MOST of them have editors of some sort, and MOST/ALL are 'professional'. I don't know who you were talking to to tell you otherwise but I'm going to disagree with that idea quite in its entirety.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I suppose that was a little whiny. Sorry about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the first SCP I saw an AWCY reference in and groaned. That sentence is not grammatically correct. The two may be linked.

My big quandary is, who is the Assistant Researcher Autism Man who counted all the fucking windows and people — making certain the effects depicted on each were entirely photorealistic, mind — without turning into a caveman?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An AWCY article I think fits the bill of AWCY. Showing humanity the truth of what's to come, as they depict it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I liked it. I didn't like it when I was reading it, though - I initially also didn't understand the connection between a nuclear detonation's painting and a transfiguration. By the end, I did. Normally, I tend to have a knee-jerk reaction to invoking GOI's, like you referenced earlier, but I don't know, this one kind of fits for me. So, +1, nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wouldn't like this regardless of whether it was AWCY or not, myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it and prayed, but to no avail. -1 for it being an AWCY.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The more I think about this, the more I like it. I like the concept of being confronted with a truth so awful that it breaks you mentally and physically. It's kind of Lovecraftian in that regard.

Another thing I thought was interesting was that only viewing the painting in its entirety causes the effect, and that a tiny portion is all that needs to be obscured to prevent it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Taking semicolon discussion to PM.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. Removing downvote, but I still don't know how to approach liking it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really. I'm fairly skeptical because your experiences aren't my experiences. I'll have to find a few professional writers and ask them about it myself to verify. Thanks for informing me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If that was your intent, the connection ("devolving into terrified ape-men when confronted with the … annihilation") wasn't clear. I upvoted, but that was mostly because I hadn't been expecting an AWCY and was surprised when I got to the end because in retrospect I totally should have seen it coming.

I also have some fairly grave suspension-of-disbelief issues w/r/t skull-restructuring (it seems highly likely to me that "painful" would be the least of one's problems, considering how harmful intracranial pressure can be in less extreme circumstances). I mean, if somebody wanted to vote against it for that alone, I wouldn't hold it against them.

There is some knee-jerk downvoting here at the SCPF (and occasionally some regular jerk-jerk downvoting), but you probably shouldn't mention it when it's your own article under discussion. Being disappointed that an article wasn't better-received is totally understandable and maybe even expected; accusing everyone of bad-faith downvoting because the article wasn't better-received is probably mistaken. Even if it isn't mistaken, saying so 1) makes you look pouty and entitled and 2) isn't going to change any votes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um, nearly everyone professional ever has misinformed me, then. There is a good reason you can read a few novels, magazine articles, essays, etc. and still never find one semi-colon. Semi-colons aren't inherently wrong but using one almost always indicates you could re-write a better sentence with no semi-colon required.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Semi-colons are valued and respected members of the punctuation community. They are not, as you suggest, to be avoided at all costs, and whoever told you they were was misinformed.

Now let's discuss your ellipsis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't dislike this, but I don't care for the AWCY connection. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was flopping between votes for a bit before I decided on a no-vote. The writing is well-executed, the concept is decent, and you've managed to do an AWCY? piece that actually incorporates art and something that isn't purely "explosions and mystical artsy sayings", but ultimately it doesn't grab me enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm honestly disappointed that this one wasn't better-recieved, I do feel like people are responding negatively out of a knee-jerk reaction to the basic premise without really considering what it's trying to say. On the other hand, I suppose it is a pretty silly concept. I just liked the idea of a room-full of art snobs devolving into terrified ape-men when confronted with the inevitable annihilation of their precious civilization.

I'll take it down in the morning if it's still in the negative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love semicolons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All that is great, whatever, but you did NOT just suggest someone use a semi-colon… Use a period if possible. If that is not possible, rewrite the whole damn sentence or even paragraph before using a semi-colon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writing is decent for the most part and it all fits together pretty well, but honestly it just kinda bored me overall. Sorry, but it's a downvote for me.

Edit: I've softened up to this one just enough to make it a no-vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. No, cavemen. Pay attention :>
  2. Even with that point corrected, that's a bit of a simplistic way to look at it. Yes, that's what it boils down to, but not all foods are best when boiled. Pay no attention to the partially mangled metaphor in the corner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a painting that turns people into Super Mutants? No thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No problem, reads fine now. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okeedoke. Sorry, not at all my area of expertise. Thanks for the help!

(Though "strike" in that sense meant "attack", not "impact")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'Nuclear warhead strike' makes even less sense. Advise replacing 'a nuclear warhead strike on' with 'the detonation of a nuclear weapon above'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nuclear warhead it is.
